text: of ceremonies in rites of passage or formative experiences. This meaning again survives in a series of modern words like ‘premier’, ‘prince’, ‘principle’, ‘priest’, ‘primordial’, or ‘primitive’. Linguists emphasize that the original meaning implied a concrete, spatial rather than temporal sequence, which is in full congruence with van Gennep’s framework, where he places the spatial or territorial passage before all other ritual passages in the presentation of the ethnographic material (Chapter 2 of his book, we remember, is entirely dedicated to the ritual passing of spatial thresholds). Even further, *per derivatives also capture the idea of ‘birth’, or to ‘bring into the world’, most importantly in ‘parent’, but also as in the Italian partorire (give birth to); possibly even ‘father’ (pater). Szakolczai finally suggests that a derivate word, ‘part’, even captures the background horizon of any experience-passage: the ‘home’, from which and towards which any passage is performed as a taking part in something, or as an experience of ‘participation’. In short, the related etymology is extremely dense and Turner was certainly on the right track when opening up the analysis in this direction. Turner realized that liminality served not only to identify the importance of in-between periods, but also to understand the human reactions to liminal experiences: the way in which personality was shaped by liminality, the sudden foregrounding of agency, and the sometimes dramatic tying together of thought and experience. Turner came to identify his own project with the philosophy of Dilthey for this very reason (see Turner’s two most important discussions of Dilthey in 1982: 12–19; 1988: 84–97). This was indeed an important intellectual encounter, made late in Turner’s life (see also the discussion by Szakolczai 2004: 69–72; here Szakolczai explicitly develops the argument towards an ‘experiential sociology’). Turner immediately perceived an affinity between his own work and Dilthey’s, arguing that it takes an anthropologist to understand the significance of Dilthey (Turner 1985b: 210). Via Dilthey, Turner returned to the perhaps greatest question of modern philosophy: namely experience and how to understand experience. On the one hand, experience had been a cornerstone of both Cartesian and Kantian thought, hence of modern philosophy as such. On the other hand, there was something deeply problematic about modern philosophy and its hold on experience – or rather, its non-hold. And this was exactly what Dilthey had realized. In a certain sense, Turner was therefore trying to take up the ‘impossible’ Kantian task that Durkheim had pursued, namely of providing the a priori with ethnographic backing. Turner was himself not very explicit about what he was involved in, and to an extent it remained at an embryonic stage. As he wrote shortly before he died, ‘I don’t want to become involved in Dilthey’s philosophical speculations’ (1988: 90). But involved he was, and there is every reason to make explicit what was going on in order to pursue it further: The basis of knowledge does indeed have a social foundation, but the ‘social’ itself is no longer that abstract, reified collective consciousness represented and reproduced Liminality and the Modern 88 by undifferentiated primitives in the Durkhemian weltbild: it is the social drama performed by individuals who in the ritual act bring to the fore, and elaborate, cultural essentials. The exercise of tying together ethnography and philosophy has now been dramatically redefined. And this again was done much more in the spirit of van Gennep than in the spirit of Durkheim. Against the Kantian framework, Dilthey maintained that knowledge is based on structures of experience that are at once cognitive, affective and volitional – all of which contribute to the ‘form’ of the actual performance itself (as nicely summed up by Turner himself, 1988: 55). Following Turner’s own suggestions, it seems meaningful to suggest that much of our conceptual thought derives from human elaborations of in-betweenness. The importance of Dilthey rested with his revolt against Kantian and neo- Kantian thinking. In Kant’s worldview, the world itself is chaotic and unordered, and it takes a transcendental mind to make sense of it; but even this is a hopeless task as we can never know the ‘thing-in-itself’. Dilthey intuited that human experience is not chaotic and random, to be ‘constructed’ and ordered by the abstract categories of the transcendental mind; rather, experience has a structure of its own. But what was the nature of this structure? What were the ‘structures of experience’ that Dilthey kept insisting on? Elaborating van Gennep’s work, Turner identified these with the triadic, sequential and processual structure of rites of passage at the core of anthropology. Turner actually solved these problems, only his efforts were buried in a few posthumous essays. As succinctly put by Szakolczai (2009: 147), ‘[t]he solution is the recognition that the sequential order of a rite of passage is the structure of lived experience’. This dialogue across empirical ethnography, anthropological concept formation and continental philosophy is of utmost relevance, far more promising and deep-reaching than Durkheim’s forced empiricism – and we need to give it more attention than has so far been the case. The Kant-Durkheim genealogy, in all its later, various off-shots into nominalism, structuralism, Marxism and social constructivism, needs to be replaced by a Nietzsche-van Gennep/Dilthey-Turner genealogy. This has importance far beyond the anthropology of performance; it has importance for how we approach thought, knowledge and experience and how they interconnect in critical junctures. And we can now attempt to tie together the knots in a more comprehensive analytical framework, singling out, more schematically and as directly as possible, the possible spatio-temporal dimensions of liminality and the complementary concepts that serve to energize its analytical and diagnostic value. Chapter 4 Dimensions of Liminality This chapter attempts to pave further analytical ground, with and beyond Victor Turner, towards the application of liminality within a context of modernity, and in three ways: first, by proposing a descriptive model and a ‘typology’ that identifies various spatial and temporal dimensions of liminal situations; second, by suggesting a series of complementary concepts that facilitate a meaningful application of the concept
